items.q: What four growth strategies does Ansoff's Matrix identify?
items.correct: Market penetration (existing product, existing market), market development (existing product, new market), product development (new product, existing market), diversification (new product, new market).
items.misconception: Ansoff's Matrix is about analysing competitors.
items.random_wrong: Ansoff's Matrix shows market share against profit.
items.why: Students confuse Ansoff (growth strategy) with Porter's Five Forces (competition) or BCG Matrix (portfolio). Ansoff is specifically about the four product/market combinations for growth.

items.q: Why does a company's share price change?
items.correct: Share prices reflect investors' expectations about future profits — driven by company performance, sector trends, economic conditions, and market sentiment.
items.misconception: Share prices go up when a company makes more money this year.
items.random_wrong: Share prices are set by the government each morning.
items.why: Markets are forward-looking. A company can report record profits and see shares fall if results were worse than expected. Students miss the expectations-driven nature of pricing.

items.q: What are Porter's two main generic strategies for competitive advantage?
items.correct: Cost leadership (being the lowest-cost producer) and differentiation (offering unique value customers will pay more for) — Porter argues firms should choose one or the other.
items.misconception: Porter says businesses should pursue both low cost and differentiation at once.
items.random_wrong: Porter's strategies are about marketing channels.
items.why: Porter's central warning is against being 'stuck in the middle'. Students assume more strategies = better; Porter argues choosing one focus is more effective than trying both.

items.q: Porter's three generic strategies are cost leadership, differentiation and:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Focus. “Diversification” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The 'Blue Ocean' concept was proposed by:
items.why: Correct answer: Kim and Mauborgne. “Porter” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many generic strategies did Porter identify?
items.why: The real figure is 3 strategies. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
